coredata.dc:title: Egg yolk-derived carbon: Achieving excellent fluorescent carbon dots and high performance lithium-ion batteries
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Shuguang
Wang, Haifeng
Zhang, Rui
Zhao, Liang
Wu, Xinglong
Xie, Haiming
Zhang, Jingping
Sun, Haizhu
coredata.dc:description: A sustainable and green one-arrow-two-hawks strategy is developed to cyclically prepare nitrogen-doped carbon materials from egg yolk. Firstly, nitrogen-doped fluorescent carbon dots (N-CDs) are obtained from the supernatant product via hydrothermal reaction. The resulted N-CDs show excellent water solubility and desired photoluminescence properties with the relative quantum yield (QY) of about 35%. A bulk fluorescent nanocomposite fabricated by using the prepared N-CDs exhibits excellent processibility, showing its potential applications in a wide range of fields. The bottom product via hydrothermal reaction is used as lithium-ion batteries anodes after carbonization, exhibiting desired electrochemical performances such as the capacity of 711.6 mA h g−1 after 100 cycles at 100 mA g−1. Moreover, the bottom product can be reused as the carbon source for the next round synthesis of N-CDs. N-CDs obtained from each round still exhibit a strong blue emission under UV illumination (365 nm) with averaged relative QY of about 26%. To the best of our knowledge, the strategy to obtain N-CDs with high relative QY through cycle hydrothermal reaction is seldom reported, which is significant to improve the utilization of original materials and decrease the cost of synthesis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Egg yolk
Fluorescent carbon dots
Lithium-ion battery
Nitrogen-doped
Sustainable

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis process of the N-CDs and anode materials for LIBs.

(a), (b) UV–vis absorption and PL spectra of S1 as prepared and after six months under different excitation wavelengths. Insets are photos of S1 as prepared and after six months under daylight (left) and UV light (right) irradiated by 365 nm light. (c), (d) Fluorescence decay profile of S1 as prepared and after six months.
(a) XRD pattern of S1. (b) TEM image of S1. (c) HRTEM image of S1.
(a) FTIR spectrum of S1. (b) XPS survey spectrum of S1. (c) High resolution XPS pattern of C 1s and (d) N 1s of S1.

(a) Comparison of the CD QY reported in this work with those reported in the literatures using biological macromolecules as carbon source. (b) QY of S1, S2, S3, S4 and S5. Inset is the optical photograph of the bulk S1/PVA nanocomposites.
(a) XRD pattern, (b) Raman spectra, (c) XPS survey spectrum, High-resolution (d) C 1s, (e) N1s and (f) O 1s of B1.
(a) SEM image and (b) TEM image of B1.
(a) Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm and (b) pore size distribution profiles of B1.

(a) Charge-discharge profiles of the B1 electrode for different cycles at a current density of 100 mA g−1. (b) CV curves of the B1 electrode at the first 5 cycles. (c) Cycle performance and CE of the B1 electrode at 100 mA g−1, the inset optical image shows the light-emitting diode by the current LIBs. (d) Rate performance of the B1 electrode. (e) Cycling performance of the B1 at a current density of 500 mA g−1.
Nyquist plots for the B1 electrode at different cycles.

head.ce:title: Egg yolk-derived carbon: Achieving excellent fluorescent carbon dots and high performance lithium-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, National & Local United Engineering Laboratory for Power Batteries, Northeast Normal University, Changchun, 130024, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National & Local United Engineering Laboratory for Power Batteries

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A sustainable and green one-arrow-two-hawks strategy is developed to cyclically prepare nitrogen-doped carbon materials from egg yolk. Firstly, nitrogen-doped fluorescent carbon dots (N-CDs) are obtained from the supernatant product via hydrothermal reaction. The resulted N-CDs show excellent water solubility and desired photoluminescence properties with the relative quantum yield (QY) of about 35%. A bulk fluorescent nanocomposite fabricated by using the prepared N-CDs exhibits excellent processibility, showing its potential applications in a wide range of fields. The bottom product via hydrothermal reaction is used as lithium-ion batteries anodes after carbonization, exhibiting desired electrochemical performances such as the capacity of 711.6 mA h g−1 after 100 cycles at 100 mA g−1. Moreover, the bottom product can be reused as the carbon source for the next round synthesis of N-CDs. N-CDs obtained from each round still exhibit a strong blue emission under UV illumination (365 nm) with averaged relative QY of about 26%. To the best of our knowledge, the strategy to obtain N-CDs with high relative QY through cycle hydrothermal reaction is seldom reported, which is significant to improve the utilization of original materials and decrease the cost of synthesis.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based materials have attracted much attention because of their unique properties such as non-toxicity, low cost and biocompatibility [1–4]. These characteristics make them have extensive applications in biomedical materials, energy storage and catalysis [5–9]. Among kinds of carbon-based materials, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have become one of the hottest topics due to their exceptional advantages, such as water solubility, excellent chemical stability, and luminescent capability [1–3]. In addition, with the rapid development of the global economy, it is important to develop efficient energy conversion and storage devices to meet the requirements of the current society development. As known, lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have found applications in portable electronics, hybrid electric vehicles and microelectronic devices [6–8]. Recently, carbon-based nanotubes and graphitic foam were proved to be one of the most promising anode materials for LIBs because of their high conductivity and easy N-doping [7,9]. Besides, Si-based materials and hierarchical porous carbon have emerged as leading anode candidates in view of their abundance and sustainability [10–12]. However, it still remains a challenge to develop a smart route to simultaneously obtain CDs and anode materials for LIBs via a simple process.

Either the fluorescent or the electrochemical properties will be largely improved if nitrogen element is doped into the carbon matrix [13]. For example, Yang and co-workers prepared N-doped CDs (N-CDs) with strong fluorescence through pyrolysis of citric acid and ethylenediamine [5]. N-doping is one of the most effective ways to enhance the emission of the CDs, which can induce an upward shift in the Fermi level and a change in optical properties [14]. Huang's group synthesized N-doped porous graphene sheets, which showed impressive reversible capacity as LIBs anode materials [15]. The presence of N atom in a proper content will enhance the conductivity of material, and introduce more reactive sites for Li-ions [15–17]. Another important factor to be considered is the selection of carbon sources. Nowadays, a lot of original materials such as citric acid [5], carbon fibers [18], benzenediol isomers [19], glycine [20], food waste [21], urine [22], dried grass [23], potatoes [24], biomass-derived carbons [25] and willow leaves [26] etc. have been chosen to prepare CDs or carbon anodes. Among these materials, biological macromolecules are ideal original materials for green synthesis of functional carbon materials because of their renewability, abundance and sustainability. Besides, the biological materials are often nitrogen containing materials and hence can be used as both carbon and nitrogen sources to realize homogeneous N-doping. For example, Li et al. reported an alkali-assisted electrochemical method to prepare CDs with quantum yield (QY) of ∼12.0% [4]. Chen and co-workers produced CDs with QY of ∼5.96% using rapid plasma though pyrolysis of eggs [27]. However, most of these methods suffer from drawbacks such as low QY, low yield, or are involved in using the special and sophisticated equipment, which limits their abroad applications. Therefore, developing simple, low cost and green synthesis methods to obtain N-CDs is highly desired.

In this work, a sustainable and green one-arrow-two-hawks strategy is developed to realize the recycling preparation of fluorescent N-CDs with high relative QY and LIBs by using egg yolk as both carbon and nitrogen source. Eggs play an important role in our daily life because of their abundant protein with a lot of nitrogen and carbon elements. Fig. 1 gives the synthetic procedure of N-CDs and LIBs. The supernatant (S1) with relative QY of ∼35% was obtained from the solution. At the same time, B1 resulted from the bottom product (BP) after further carbonization was employed as anodes for LIBs, which exhibited good electrochemical performances, such as high capacity and long cycle life (601.0 mA h g−1 even after 300 cycles at 500 mA g−1). Meanwhile, BP can also be reused as the carbon source for the next round synthesis of N-CDs (Fig. 1). N-CDs obtained from each round still showed excellent water solubility and desired PL properties with the averaged relative QY of about 26%. To the best of our knowledge, there are few reports about N-doped carbon materials derived from single biomaterials source, which repeatedly undergoes hydrothermal reaction for many times to obtain N-CDs and at the same time, can be used as anodes for LIBs. This will undoubtedly improve the utilization of the original materials and decrease the cost of synthesis.

In a typical synthesis procedure, the boiled egg yolk was put into vacuum oven and dried for 24 h. Then, dried egg yolk (20 g) was put into a tube furnace and heated up to 300 °C under N2 atmosphere for 3 h at a heating rate of 5 °C min−1. After cooled down to room temperature, a mixture of concentrated H2SO4 and HNO3 (3:1, v/v) was used to decompose the above samples at 120 °C for 24 h to get the intermediate products. Then, the products were added into distilled water (250 mL) under magnetic stirring and the N-doped precursors were obtained after centrifugation (10000 rpm).

5 g of N-doped precursors were dispersed in 160 mL purified water under magnetic stirring. The mixture (40 mL) was stirred for 30 min and then transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, followed by a heat treatment at 300 °C for 12 h (Fig. 1). Then the bottom product (BP) was removed by centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 4 min. The supernatant was further filtration with a cylinder filtration membrane filter (0.22 μm) to obtain a transparent S1 solution. The BP obtained after centrifuged could be further used to obtain S2, S3, S4, and S5, etc., followed the similar process to S1.

BP was employed to prepare N-doping anode material for LIBs. Firstly, BP and borax were mixed using a mortar with the weight ratio of 1:3. Then the mixture was calcined at 700 °C for 2 h under N2 atmosphere. After cooled down to room temperature, the resultant product was repeatedly washed by using 0.1 M HCl and distilled water and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 5 min. At last, the product was dried at 60 °C for 24 h, which was named as B1.

The active material powders (B1), acetylene black and polyvinylidenefluoride (PVDF) in a weight ratio of 8:1:1 in N-methyl-2-pyrrolidione (NMP) were used to form homogeneous slurry. The resulted slurry was pasted onto copper foils and dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 12 h. The loading mass of the carbon on the anodes is about 1.1 mg cm−2. Pure lithium foil was used as counter electrode. The electrolyte employed was 1.0 M LiPF6 in 1:1 (v/v) ethylene carbonate (EC) and dimethyl carbonate (DMC), the amount of which added in each coin cell was ∼160 μL. The coin-type cells were assembled in an Ar-filled glovebox. The cells of LIBs were tested by the LAND CT2001A battery test system with potential ranged from 0.01 to 3 V at room temperature. Cyclic voltammetric (CV) tests were carried out on a CHI750E electrochemical station at a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1.

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained using a fluorescent spectrophotometer (FLSPECTOROPHOTOMET F-4600). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU UV-2550). The relative QYs of N-CDs were measured using quinine sulphate in a 0.1 M H2SO4 aqueous solution (QY is 0.54) as a reference. The crystalline structure of products was recorded by using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Rigaku P/max 2200VPC), using Cu-K (λ = 0.15406 nm) radiation. Raman scattering spectra were studied with a JY HR-800 Lab Ramconfocal Raman microscope. The morphology of the samples was studied by the scanning electron microscope (SEM, XL 30ESEM-FEG, FEI Company) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2010F). The chemical composition was determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermal ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with Al Kα radiation). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was recorded on a Nicolet Magna 560 FT-IR spectrophotometer.

The quantum yield of CDs were achieved by the following equation:
ϕ =
ϕ R
×
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
n 2
n R 2
where, the subscript “R” refers to quinine sulfate (the reference fluorophore). Φ and I represent the quantum yield and integrated PL intensities. A and n refer to the absorbance at the excitation wavelength and refractive index of the solution, respectively. The quinine sulfate (ΦR = 0.54) was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 while the N-CDs were dissolved in deionized water. In order to minimize re-absorption effects, absorbance was kept below 0.10 at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm.

UV–vis absorption spectra of S1 prepared at 300 °C is shown in Fig. 2 a. There is a shoulder peak at about 290 nm, which is assigned to n-π* transition of the C=O bond [28]. The PL properties of the as-prepared S1 were examined by using different excitation wavelengths. Excitation-dependent PL behaviour of S1 is shown in Fig. 2a. The PL peaks show red-shift from 386 to 450 nm when the excitation wavelength shifts from 270 to 410 nm. This phenomenon is ascribed to the existence of surface state, which affects the band gap of S1 [5,29]. This excitation-dependent PL behaviour indicates their potential application in multi-colour imaging. The photos of S1 with a strong blue emission under UV illumination (365 nm) are shown in the inset of Fig. 2a. Its relative QY is about 35% which is hardly decreased even after six months storage (Figs. 2b and 32%). These results not only prove that S1 has a desired fluorescence properties but also a good stability.

Fluorescent lifetime is a characteristic period that the CDs remain in the excited state prior to returning to the ground state [30]. Fluorescent decay curves of S1 as prepared and after six months are presented in Fig. 2c and d which is fitted by using the following equations:
(1)
Y
( t )
=
A 1
exp
(
−
t
τ 1
)
+
A 2
exp
(
−
t
τ 2
)
where A1 and A2 are the fractional contributions of decay time of τ1 and τ2. The lifetimes of S1 are 1.3 and 7.0 ns with amplitudes of 79% and 21%, respectively. The shorter lifetime comes from the conjugated structures while the longer one results from the surface traps [30]. The lifetimes of S1 after six months are shown in Fig. 2d (τ1 = 2.4 ns and τ2 = 8.3 ns). Furthermore, the average lifetimes of S1 are calculated to be 2.50 ns and 4.88 ns for the samples as prepared and after six months, respectively. These results further prove that N-CDs are stable, which is important for their applications.

In Fig. 3 a, XRD curve shows a narrow peak centered at 26°, which is characteristic of graphite with a (002) layer spacing, confirming their graphite-like structure [31]. The morphology and structure of S1 are further characterized by TEM. The shape of S1 is spherical and there is no apparent aggregation (Fig. 3b). The HRTEM image reveals the discernible crystal structures with a lattice space of 0.32 nm, corresponding to the graphite (002) carbon (Fig. 3c), which is in good consistent with the result of XRD [32]. The results show that S1 possesses high crystallinity, which might be in favour of the excellent luminescent properties.

In Fig. 4 a, FTIR spectrum of S1 exhibits distinct absorption bands at 3404 and 1712 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of O-H and C=O [23,33,34]. These results confirm the existence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of S1, implying its good biocompatibility. The small peaks at 1634, 3262 and 1400 cm−1 are assigned to the C=C, N-H and C-N stretching vibration [34], which reveal the existence of N-containing groups. The C-N bond has been proved to be the reason for the enhanced QY of the N-CDs [34]. The peaks in the range of 1000–1400 cm−1 are C-O and C-H stretching vibration, indicating that the surface of S1 is partially oxidized [33,34]. XPS was performed to analyse the elemental composition and chemical status of the prepared N-CDs. There are three characteristic peaks at about 283.3, 401.6 and 531.3 eV in the XPS spectrum (Fig. 4b), corresponding to C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively. The atomic ratios of C, O and N are 58.76, 39.65 and 1.59 atom%, respectively. This also confirms that N is in the framework of S1. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1s is fitted with four components, including 284.3 (C-C), 284.8 (C=C), 285.7 (C-N, C-O) and 288.3 eV (C=O), which confirms the presence of N and O-rich functional groups again (Fig. 4c) [23,34,35]. The N 1s peaks at 401.2 (pyrrolic-N) and 402 eV (graphitic-N) are shown in Fig. 4d [23,36,37]. These results exhibit that nitrogen and oxygen containing groups are on the surface of S1. The presence of the abundant oxygen-containing groups and hydrophilic groups makes N-CDs stable in aqueous system, without precipitation even after several months (Fig. 2b) [21]. These functional groups also increase their potential applications in many fields, such as drug delivery, biological imaging and ion detection, etc.

The relative QYs in this work are compared with other classic literatures by using biological macromolecules as carbon source to synthesize CDs (Fig. 5 a). The results show that the QY of the N-CDs in this work is among the highest values for the CDs derived from biomass materials. Especially, the BP obtained after centrifuged can be reused as the carbon source for the next round to obtain S2, S3, S4, and S5 (Fig. 1). The relative QYs of the S2, S3, S4 and S5 are shown in Fig. 5b, which is 25.06%, 26.42%, 20.82% and 18.98%, respectively. The BP can be recyclable for many times, which will undoubtedly improve the utilization of the original materials and decrease the cost of synthesis. In addition, we prepared the bulk nanocomposite by processing S1 with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), which is shown in the inset of Fig. 5b. The nanocomposite exhibits a strong blue emission under UV lamp (365 nm). As shown in Fig. 5b, the nanocomposites can be processed into various shapes and exhibit their good processibility, which is beneficial for their practical applications.

To illustrate the advantages of the B1, lithium-ion storage properties were investigated by using it as anode materials. Firstly, the BP and borax were mixed with the weight ratio of 1:3. Then the mixture was calcined at 700 °C for 2 h under N2 atmosphere to obtain B1. Further structural information of B1 can be obtained from XRD, Raman, XPS, SEM, and TEM measurements. As shown in Fig. 6 a, B1 has a broad peak at about 23°, indicating an amorphous carbon phase [6]. Raman spectrum was used to characterize the graphitization degree of B1 (Fig. 6b). There are two peaks at 1336 and 1584 cm−1, corresponding to D- and G-bands. That the intensity ratio (ID/IG) is 1.125 indicates a high graphitization degree, which will improve the conductivity and is in favour of the rate performance. XPS measurement was performed to examine the chemical composition and bond types of B1 (Fig. 6c). A typical high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s for B1 is shown in Fig. 6d. The peaks located at about 284.2, 285.0, 286.3 and 288.6 eV are ascribed to C=C/C-C, C-OH, C-O-C and C=O groups, respectively [38–40]. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum is shown in Fig. 6e. The peaks at binding energies of 397.6, 399.9 and 401.7 eV are attributed to the pyridine-like N, pyrrole-like N and graphite-like N, respectively [6,15]. The nitrogen with higher electronegativity compared to carbon atoms will generate more favourable binding sites for Li-ions [41]. As to the chemical bond types of oxygen, the three peaks at 530.2, 531.9 and 533.2 eV (Fig. 6f) result from pyridone O, carbonyl O (C=O) and ether or phenol O (C-O-C or C-OH) [40]. The atomic ratios of N to O are 1.76% and 14.65%, respectively. Therefore, it is concluded that O-enriched carbon materials with N-doping have been simultaneously obtained by using yolk as the single carbon source. B1 is composed of many incompact balls, leading to a loose structure (Fig. 7 a). These characteristics might increase the contact area of the electrolyte and facilitate it diffusion. TEM image confirms that B1 has a layered structure (Fig. 7b). The unique features would provide a better access for the electrolyte deep into the entire anode via the pores, which is a key role in high specific capacity at high rates.

The large BET surface area (370.6 m2 g−1) and pore volume (0.73 cm3 g−1) of B1 are illustrated by N2 adsorption–desorption analysis. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the curve exhibits a typical type II isotherm with a H3 hysteresis loop. The isotherm shows high uptake at a low relative pressure of 0–0.1, showing that B1 sample possesses substantial micropores. The distinct hysteresis loop shows that B1 possesses the typical mesopores structure (Fig. 8a). The average size of pores is about 14 nm, and most pore size is located at 5 nm (Fig. 8b). These features are significant for the ionic diffusion during the electrochemical process, resulting in desired electrochemical properties.

To test their lithium storage properties, B1 was used as anode material for LIBs (Fig. 9 ). The charge-discharge curves of the prepared B1 at 100 mA g−1 for different cycle numbers are shown in Fig. 9a. It is observed that the first discharge capacity of B1 is 1234 mA h g−1 with a Coulombic efficiency (CE) of 59.6%. This value is higher than that (48.4%) of N-carbon nanofibers [42]. The low CE may be due to the irreversible lithiation behaviour [43]. The discharge capacity is higher for the first cycle than that for the second cycle (739 mA h g−1), resulting from the formation of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) film and irreversible Li-ion insertion [43]. However, the CE increased dramatically and stable at ∼98% after the following cycles. At the end of the 100th cycle, the delivered capacity still remains 711.6 mA h g−1. CV of B1 electrode at 0.1 mV s−1 within a potential range of 0.01–3.0 V is shown in Fig. 9b. In the first cycle, there is an obvious reduction peak at about 1.2 V, which is indiscernible in the subsequent cycles. This is caused by the decomposition of the electrolyte and the formation of SEI layer on the electrode surface [15,42,44]. The reduction peak at about 0.5 V is ascribed to the diffusion-limited lithium storage in defects (such as oxygenic functional groups). Correspondingly, the overvoltage located at 1.2 V is ascribed to the lithium extraction from defects during the anodic scan [15]. The CV curves become quite similar after the second cycle, which confirms a good reversible and stable state of B1.

The cycling performance of B1 electrode was studied at 100 mA g−1 (Fig. 9c). As illustrated in Fig. 9c, no obvious decline is observed in the charge capacity. The capacity still stabilizes at 711.6 mA h g−1 even after 100 cycles. Therefore, the B1 electrode shows excellent cycle stability. The inset of Fig. 9c shows that the resulted LIBs can easily power red LEDs. The above results demonstrate that B1 is a promising electrode material for high performance LIBs. The rate performance is another important parameter for LIBs. The capacities of B1 are 663.4, 488.1, 408.4, 321.0 and 259.6 mA h g−1 at 100, 300, 500, 1000 and 1500 mA g−1, respectively (Fig. 9d). This good rate performance is more excellent than nitrogen-doped porous graphene sheets [15]. The capacity can completely recover to its initial value even after cycle of discharging/charging at different current densities. The cycling performance of B1 under the current density of 500 mA g−1 is investigated. As shown in Fig. 9e, the capacity of B1 increases gradually and keeps stable at 601.0 mA h g−1 even after 300 cycles with almost 100% CE, which is much higher than porous MoS2-reduced graphene oxide and porous CuO nanotubes/graphene [45,46]. The gradual increase of capacity upon cycling may be caused by the electrochemical activation process, which can be ascribed to an improved accessibility of Li-ions into the inner area of the electrode with the increase of cycle numbers. More activated sites will be used for Li storage after activation process [15]. Therefore, the capacity goes up during the initial cycling, but it stabilized quickly after 100 cycles [15,36,42,47].

Electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) was used to explore the electrochemical kinetics of the electrode reaction. The Nyquist plots at high and medium frequency region are attributed to the contact resistance and charge transfer resistance, respectively. In addition, the straight line in the low frequency region of the EIS corresponds to the diffusion of Li+ in the electrode. As shown in Fig. 10 a, the diameters semicircles of the samples become smaller with the increase of cycle number, which indicates a decreased contact and charge transfer resistance. It is believed that this phenomenon results from better contact between the electrolyte and active material, and the permeation of electrolyte into the porous structure. Therefore, it enlarges the interface area of electrolyte and electrode as reaction sites, and finally reduces the internal resistance to promote the electrode kinetics [48]. This also demonstrates that the micropores and mesoporous channels in the electrode favour a short diffusion distance for quick electron and ion transfer. All results ensure the increase in capacity and the excellent cycling stability of the B1-based electrode.

In summary, a sustainable and green one-arrow-two-hawks strategy is developed to realize recycling preparation of N-CDs with high QY retention and LIBs by using egg yolk as both carbon and nitrogen source. The relative QY of the obtained N-CDs was as high as 35%. As anode materials for LIBs, the obtained B1 possessed excellent electrochemical properties and good cycling stability. Besides demonstrating their outstanding performance as fluorescent CDs and LIBs, the new methodology to synthesize N-doped carbon materials via a natural source is low cost and environment friendly, showing great potentials in the field of bio-imaging, display and energy storage.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial supports from the NSFC (21574018, 51433003, and 21573036), Jilin Provincial Education Department (543), Jilin Provincial Key Laboratory of Advanced Energy Materials (Northeast Normal University) and Changbai Mountain Scholar Project by Education Department of the Jilin Province are gratefully acknowledged.
